caveats: a: The occupational proxy includes government and construction roles outside this NAICS industry.
a: No NAICS-specific task-time or wage-by-task dataset was located.
rho: This is a five-year judgment rather than a measured adoption rate.
rho: State and locality rules vary, and the source evidence does not measure AI deployment.
e: NAICS establishment definitions do not identify recurring revenue, normalized EBITDA, or transferability.
e: The supplied firm-count estimate is not re-estimated here.

s5: Occupational self-employment statistics are not a measure of control transfers for lower-middle-market firms.

q: The figure concerns retained implemented benefit, not revenue growth or demand effects.
d5: Occupational employment is an imperfect proxy for paid service quantity.
d5: The source includes large public-sector and construction-employer populations outside the lens.

o: Remote-inspection growth may vary sharply by property type and jurisdiction.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Typically a client-specific inspection engagement documented in a report; repeat demand may come from referral networks, property portfolios, construction milestones, or recurring customer relationships. Fees and scope are set in a pre-inspection agreement.
lens.excluded_activities: Government code-enforcement units, captive inspection departments, pest and hazardous-material inspections classified elsewhere, one-person practices without transferable operations, and businesses outside the lower-middle-market band.
lens.included_activities: Independent, external-customer building and home inspection providers in the lower-middle-market band that earn repeat or recurring outsourced work, including repeat work generated through real-estate, property-owner, insurer, or construction relationships.

a.bridge: S3 measures tasks for the broader Construction and Building Inspectors occupation, including public code inspectors and construction inspectors rather than employees of NAICS 541350. I map plan review, report drafting, image/log organization, scheduling, and client communications to AI-exposed work while retaining on-site visual examination, instrument use, safety judgment, and accountable defect calls as non-substitutable. This may overstate exposure if private inspectors spend more time on-site, and understate it if report production dominates their workflow.
caveats: The occupational proxy includes government and construction roles outside this NAICS industry.
No NAICS-specific task-time or wage-by-task dataset was located.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-motion sample from multi-inspector firms, inspection-software activity logs, and payroll by role would show the actual share of paid time in site work, report production, intake, scheduling, and plan review.
a.rationale: The occupation includes high-volume document and information tasks that can be assisted by AI, but the service requires an inspector to observe accessible property systems and make a defensible condition judgment. A minority-to-material share of wage-weighted work is therefore exposed, not the inspection itself.

d5.bridge: S2 projects total U.S. Construction and Building Inspector employment, not constant-price and constant-quality demand for private NAICS 541350, from 147,600 in 2024 to 146,500 in 2034. I convert the reported ten-year change to a five-year midpoint ratio of approximately 0.995 and widen the range for the industry's greater exposure to housing transactions. This may understate private demand if government remote-inspection savings drive the projected decline, or overstate it if residential transaction volume weakens.
caveats: Occupational employment is an imperfect proxy for paid service quantity.
The source includes large public-sector and construction-employer populations outside the lens.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year forecasts and realized series for existing-home sales, housing starts, commercial construction inspections, and NAICS 541350 receipts by service line would materially refine the demand ratio.
d5.rationale: The broad occupation projection is essentially flat to slightly declining and explicitly attributes some reduction to remote inspections, while safety and construction quality support continuing demand. A near-flat five-year constant-quality demand assumption is more defensible than extrapolating a short-term housing cycle.

caveats: NAICS establishment definitions do not identify recurring revenue, normalized EBITDA, or transferability.
The supplied firm-count estimate is not re-estimated here.
e.change_evidence: Company-level revenue concentration and customer-cohort data, state licensing rosters matched to payroll, and broker confidential-information memoranda would establish the share with transferable staff, repeat customers, and lower-middle-market earnings.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is external property-condition inspection, and it explicitly excludes government code enforcement, supporting eligibility for a substantial share of firms. Eligibility is reduced because some providers depend mainly on one-off buyer transactions, individual inspector reputation, or non-transferable referral relationships rather than a repeat outsourced-service operation.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541350 ($1,864,147,476) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541350 ($3,995,784,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $146,226 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541350); class <5: 6,523 firms, avg revenue/firm $193,991 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $30,360 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 612 firms, avg revenue/firm $827,992 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $129,581 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 305 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,798,961 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $281,537 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 143 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,816,294 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $910,250 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 23 firms, avg revenue/firm $18,547,304 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,902,653 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 17 firms, avg revenue/firm $24,508,765 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,835,622 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 40

caveats: This is a judgment about future accountability, not a measured software displacement rate.
Remote-inspection growth may vary sharply by property type and jurisdiction.
o.change_evidence: Claims data, state practice-rule changes, lender or insurer acceptance policies, and adoption data for remote or self-service inspection products would reveal how much service quantity can be eliminated or supplied entirely through software.
o.rationale: The durable core requires a person to examine a site, use testing equipment, interpret defects, and deliver a written finding under defined professional practices. Remote capture and AI can remove some visits or tasks, but most demand should continue to require an accountable inspector for physical observation and liability-bearing judgment.

q.bridge: S5 is an association model agreement for home-inspection members, not a survey of NAICS 541350 economics. It identifies a separately stated fee and payment timing, which I use as evidence of per-engagement contracted pricing and translate to partial, rather than full, operator retention of internal efficiency benefits. This may overstate retention where local referral competition quickly lowers fees and understate it where capacity is scarce or scope expands.
caveats: The agreement is a member resource and is not representative pricing evidence.
The figure concerns retained implemented benefit, not revenue growth or demand effects.
q.change_evidence: A panel of quote-level fees, inspection duration, report turnaround, and gross margin from firms adopting AI report tools would measure retention and any subsequent repricing.
q.rationale: A pre-inspection agreement can preserve some savings within a job fee, especially for report-production and administration improvements. However, local price competition, agent and customer expectations, and faster turnaround can pass benefits to buyers through lower fees or more included services over time.

caveats: This is a five-year judgment rather than a measured adoption rate.
State and locality rules vary, and the source evidence does not measure AI deployment.
rho.change_evidence: Vendor adoption data from Spectora, HomeGauge, and Inspector Toolbelt; insurer underwriting requirements; and interviews with state-licensed inspection firms would test which workflows are in production and whether reviewer time falls.
rho.rationale: Report drafting, image labeling, appointment intake, and plan summarization can be deployed quickly, but implementation is constrained by state practice rules, proof of work quality, client liability, and the need for a credentialed person to validate findings. The base assumes implementation of many back-office and report-assist uses, not autonomous field inspection.

caveats: No NAICS-specific longitudinal ownership-transfer series was obtained.
Occupational self-employment statistics are not a measure of control transfers for lower-middle-market firms.
s5.change_evidence: Closed-transaction records from BizBuySell and regional business brokers, state license ownership-change filings, and seller interviews stratified by employee count would directly measure five-year qualifying control transfers.
s5.rationale: A qualifying transfer is plausible for firms with multiple credentialed inspectors, documented processes, and diversified referral sources, but the industry also contains owner-operator businesses whose local reputation and license are difficult to transfer. The estimate excludes shutdowns and reorganizations that do not preserve a transferable operating business.

principal_driver: Report and workflow automation can improve productive capacity while physical inspection and accountable judgment remain required.
principal_weakness: Transferability and recurring revenue are not measured in public NAICS data and may be weak for principal-dependent, transaction-driven firms.
